1: vayyehi ka'asher tamu kol hag'oi la'avor et hayarden — "when all the nation had finished crossing the Jordan." The perfect tamu marks completion; the entirety of Israel has crossed. YHWH's instruction now follows the event, not before — the twelve men were appointed before the crossing (Joshua 3:12) but their task is disclosed here. The divine speech pattern is commissioning-then-explanation.
2: qechu lakhem min ha'am shnei asar anashim — the twelve men (one per tribe) appointed in 3:12 now receive their specific task: to take twelve stones from the middle of the Jordan, from the place where the priests' feet stood. The stones come from the sacred space — the dry ground created by the divine act. They carry the site's holiness into the camp as perpetual memorial.
3: me'atsem asher amadu sham raglei hakohanim hekhinu shenem asar avanim — "from the very place where the priests' feet stood." The phrase etsem (bone/very substance) emphasizes the precise location: not near the spot but from the exact dry ground where the ark-bearers stood. The stones are material testimony to the precise location of the miracle.
4: Joshua called the twelve pre-appointed men and gave them the stone-gathering command. The sequence demonstrates Joshua's administrative competence: he pre-appointed the men before the crisis, disclosed their task at the right moment, and directed the operation efficiently. This is narrative evidence of the divine wisdom YHWH promised to give him (Joshua 1:7-8).
5: ivru lifnei aron YHWH Eloheikhem el tokh hayarden — "cross before the ark of the LORD your God into the middle of the Jordan." The twelve men must return to the dry ground of the still-standing miracle to gather their stones. The act of going back into the riverbed requires faith — the waters are held by divine power, and gathering the stones means being present in the middle of what would normally be a flooded river.
6: lema'an tihyeh zot ot beqirbekhem — "so that this may be a sign among you." Ot (sign/signal) is the covenant term for a memorial act or object: circumcision is an ot, the rainbow is an ot, the Sabbath is an ot. The stone pile is designed for the question it will provoke: mah ha'avanim ha'elleh lakhem — "what are these stones to you?" A teaching-sign that requires a question to activate.
7: vehigadtem lahem asher nikhretu mei hayarden mipnei aron berit YHWH — "tell them that the Jordan's waters were cut off before the ark of the covenant of the LORD." Nikhretu (niphal of krt) — were cut off — repeats the 3:13 verb. The cutting-off language is the covenantal verb used throughout: covenant cutting (karat berit) and water-cutting are lexically linked. The stones tell the covenant story.
8: The people obeyed; twelve stones from the Jordan's middle were brought to the camp at Gilgal. ka'asher tsivah Yehoshua — "as Joshua commanded" — the narration confirms exact compliance. The memorial-building is communal: twelve men, twelve tribes, twelve stones — a full-quorum act of covenant memory.
9: veshetem asar avanim hechin Yehoshua betokh hayarden — "Joshua also set up twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan." A second stone monument, separate from the Gilgal one: this one remains submerged, visible only at low water. The doubling of memorials means the miracle is commemorated both at the destination (Gilgal) and at the site itself (the river bottom). The verb hechin (from kwn, to establish/fix) is the same root as yakhin — established, firm.
10: vehakohanim nosim et ha'aron amdu betokh hayarden — "the priests who carried the ark stood in the midst of the Jordan." The priests stood until everything YHWH had commanded was complete — the stones taken, the people across. ka'asher tsivah Mosheh et Yehoshua — "as Moses had commanded Joshua" — Moses's preparatory instructions (Deuteronomy 27:2-8) are being fulfilled through Joshua. The chain of command (YHWH → Moses → Joshua) is maintained even after Moses's death.
11: vayyaavar aron YHWH vehakohanim lifnei ha'am — "the ark of the LORD and the priests crossed over before the people." The ark's crossing happens after all the people have crossed — the priests who entered first leave last. The ark's rear-guard position on exit mirrors its vanguard position on entry: YHWH surrounds the people front and back in the transition. The Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe (Joshua 4:12) cross armed — military readiness accompanies the sacral procession.
12: vayyaavru vnei Reuven uvnei Gad vachatsi shevet haMenasheh chamushim lifnei bnei Yisrael — the Transjordanian tribes cross armed (chamushim = equipped in battle array, or possibly by fifths — the etymology is debated). They fulfill the covenant solidarity Joshua required in Joshua 1:12-15: their own land is secured but they march with their brothers. The promise has been honored.
13: kearba'im eleph chalutsei hatsava averu lifnei YHWH lamishpat learvot Yericho — "about forty thousand armed for battle crossed before the LORD to the plains of Jericho." The number forty thousand corresponds roughly to the Transjordanian tribes' fighting force (Numbers 26:7, 18, 34). lifnei YHWH — before the LORD — the crossing is a liturgical act as well as a military one; the ark's presence means crossing before YHWH himself.
14: bayyom hahu giddel YHWH et Yehoshua be'einei kol Yisrael — "that day the LORD exalted Joshua in the sight of all Israel." The promise of Joshua 3:7 (achel gedallekha) is now fulfilled. Vayyire'u oto ka'asher yare'u et Moshe kol yemei chayav — they revered him as they had revered Moses all the days of his life. The reverence-transfer is complete: Joshua has Moses's authority because he has Moses's God. The measure of leadership is the divine presence, not personal charisma.
15: Brief divine speech: vaydabber YHWH el Yehoshua — "the LORD spoke to Joshua." The speech pattern throughout Joshua 1-5 is consistent: YHWH commands, Joshua transmits, the people obey. The divine speech has been ongoing: Joshua 1:1, Joshua 3:7, Joshua 4:1, and now Joshua 4:15. The ongoing divine address to Joshua is the authorization behind every act.
16: tsav et hakohanim nosay aron ha'edut — "command the priests who carry the ark of the testimony." Ha'edut (testimony) is the term for the law tablets inside the ark (Exodus 25:16, 21) — the ark is the container of the covenantal testimony. The priests carry both the visible ark and the invisible law it contains.
17: vayyetsav Yehoshua et hakohanim lemor alu min hayarden — "Joshua commanded the priests, 'Come up out of the Jordan.'" The terse command mirrors the priestly entry (Joshua 3:8: stand at the edge). The same obedience that produced the miracle now triggers its reversal: the priests' exit is as the priests' entry — the miracle brackets their obedience.
18: vayyehi ke'alot hakohanim nosay aron berit YHWH mitokh hayarden — "as the priests who carried the ark of the covenant of the LORD came up out of the Jordan." Nitteku (niphal of ntq) — the waters were loosed, restored to their place. The restoration is instantaneous: the moment the priests' feet leave the water, the river returns to flood stage. The miracle has no inertia — it exists exactly as long as divine power sustains it and no longer.
19: veha'am alu min hayarden be'asor lachdesh harishon — "the people came up from the Jordan on the tenth day of the first month." The date is precise: Nisan 10 — the day the Passover lamb was selected (Exodus 12:3). The land-entry is timed to the Passover preparation. Vayachanu baGilgal biqtseh mizrach Yericho — they camped at Gilgal on the east border of Jericho. Gilgal becomes the base camp for the conquest.
20: ve'et shetem asar ha'avanim ha'elleh asher lachu min hayarden hechin Yehoshua baGilgal — "Joshua set up at Gilgal the twelve stones they had taken out of the Jordan." The Gilgal monument is the visible, permanent reminder in the camp. The verb hechin (he established) echoes the earlier use: both the underwater monument (Joshua 4:9) and the Gilgal monument are hekhino — firmly placed, established. The dual memorials are twin witnesses to the same event.
21: ma ha'avanim ha'elleh — "what are these stones?" The anticipated question and the prescribed answer follow. The teaching-sign functions pedagogy: the monument raises a question the next generation will ask, creating a moment of intergenerational transmission of covenant history. The parent's answer is not a theological abstraction but a historical narrative — "Israel crossed the Jordan here on dry ground."
22: hodi'u et bneikhem lemor beyabasha avar Yisrael et hayarden hazeh — "tell your children that Israel crossed the Jordan on dry ground." The historical narration is the catechetical content: not a doctrine but an event. Bayabasha — on dry ground — repeats the key miracle-word from Joshua 3:17. The dryness of the ground in the midst of a flooded river is the point to be communicated.
23: ki hovish YHWH Eloheikhem et mei hayarden mipneikhem — "for the LORD your God dried up the waters of the Jordan before you." The explicit comparison follows: ka'asher asah YHWH Eloheikhem leyam suph asher hovish mippaneinu — as YHWH your God did to the Red Sea. The Jordan crossing is a second Exodus, and the lesson is the same: YHWH acts in history for his people by controlling the waters.
24: lema'an da'at kol amei ha'arets et yad YHWH ki chazaqah hi — "so that all the peoples of the earth may know that the hand of the LORD is powerful (chazaqah)." The adjective chazaqah (strong, mighty) echoes the repeated command of Joshua 1 — hazaq we'emats. YHWH's strength, not Joshua's, is the ground of Israel's courage. Ulema'an yire'item et YHWH Eloheikhem kol hayyamim — "and so that you may fear the LORD your God at all times." Two audiences, one miracle: the nations learn YHWH's power; Israel learns reverence. The Jordan crossing is an act of universal theodicy.
